docs.title: Hi, I'm Max
docs.text:
Welcome to my personal knowledge base — a place where I collect notes, guides, and thoughts on topics I'm exploring.

Use the navigation above to browse by topic, or head to the Blog for longer-form posts.

docs.title: Getting Started with MkDocs on GitHub
docs.text:
Setting up a mkdocs site and publishing it to GitHub Pages only takes a few commands — but the Git workflow around it trips people up at first.

git is a version control system for file. It allows you to manage different versions of your files (keeping older versions, showing what has been done in which version ...).

No worries, one needs not to learn or use git when writing stuff for your website. We only need to install it as it is necessary for mkdocs to talk to GitHub.

The following steps show how to connect your project to GitHub.

docs.title: Connect a mkdocs project to GitHub
docs.text: The following assumes that you have already a mkdocs site locally.

docs.title: 1, Create a new GitHub repository
docs.text:
Create an empty repository on GitHub (no README, no license — keep it totally empty), and link your local project to it:

On GitHub

  1. Click on your icon, then Repositories, then New

  2. Enter the name of the repository

  3. Make sure, you have no README.md, no license file

docs.title: 2. Set up git and link your local repository to GitHub
docs.text:
No we must activate git in our directory and tell git how to send our files to GitHub.

Mac Terminal 

cd <project>
git init
git branch -M main
git remote add origin git@github.com:<username>/<repo>.git

SSH key

If you're connecting via SSH (as in the example above), make sure your key is in ~/.ssh/ and added to your GitHub account (see the steps at the end).

docs.title: Deploy your site on GitHub
docs.text:
Once connected everything is very easy, especially if you don't use git for the management of the source files (mkdocs.yml, *.md files ...).

To deploy you simply build the site and instruct mkdocs to send the generated site to GitHub

mkdocs build --strict
mkdocs gh-deploy

Once run your site is visible under

<yourgitname>.github.io/<project>

Tip

If you do not want to use git for managing the versions of your source files you can forget about git. Just work on your files, add, edit or delete your files. Do not forget to build and deploy your website after changes

docs.title: Appendix: Create a ssh key for communication with GitHub (if you're new to GitHub)
docs.text:
If you haven't used git with GitHub yet, you must set a ssh key for secure communications with GitHub. This key authenticates you with GitHub and allows you to send files to your repository.

The key creation process has essentially two steps. First we generate a key locally which creates two keys, private and public. The private key stays stored on our computer. The public key will be given to GitHub.

Mac Terminal

docs.title: 1. Create a .ssh directory
docs.text:
$ cd $HOME
$ mkdir .ssh
$ cd .ssh

docs.title: 2. Generate the ssh key
docs.text: $ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "<your_email@example.com>"

docs.title: 3. Edit or create the config file
docs.text:
Use an editor to open config and enter the following information:

Host github.com
  HostName github.com
  User git
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519  # The file ssh-keygen made
  IdentitiesOnly yes

docs.title: 4. Copy the contents of the file id_ed25519.pub to the clipboard.
docs.text:
$ pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

On GitHub

docs.title: 5. Paste clipboard contents into GitHub
docs.text:
1. On GitHub click your profile icon, then Settings, then SSH and GPG keys

  2. Click on New SSH key

  3. In the Title Box, give a name for the key

  4. Paste the clipboard contents into the Text box
  5. Press the Add SSH key button

And "Voilá" we can now send files from our local repository to GitHub.

docs.title: Working with git
docs.text: Git is a version control system for your files. You can look at as a undo button for your files. Imagine you accidentally deleted a file or screwed up the file contents. Git will allow you to go back to a saved version. Let us explore how to use git, what are typical workflows and best practices are.

docs.title: Setting up git
docs.text:
The following commands bring all your files in the current directory and subdirectories under git control. The last command

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git push -u origin main

docs.title: Merging a feature branch into main
docs.text: Merging locally gives you full control before anything goes public. Here's the exact sequence using a branch called blog-update as an example.

docs.title: 1. Save your work
docs.text:
Make sure the feature branch is clean before switching away from it.

git add .
git commit -m "Finish blog updates"

docs.title: 2. Switch to main
docs.text:
You merge into the branch you're standing on.

git checkout main

docs.title: 3. Sync with GitHub (optional but recommended)
docs.text:
If you've made any changes directly on GitHub, pull them first so your local main is up to date.

git pull origin main

docs.title: 4. Merge
docs.text:
git merge blog-update

Two outcomes are possible:

  • Fast-forward — main hasn't changed since you branched off, so Git just moves the pointer forward. Clean and instant.
  • Recursive merge / conflict — both branches have new changes. Git combines them or asks you to resolve conflicts.

docs.title: 5. Push
docs.text:
git push origin main

This is usually what triggers the GitHub Actions workflow to rebuild and deploy the site.

docs.title: Resolving merge conflicts
docs.text:
If the merge fails with "Automatic merge failed", don't panic. It just means the same line was edited in both branches.

  1. Run git status to see which files are conflicted.
  2. Open each file — look for <<<<<<< HEAD and >>>>>>> blog-update markers.
  3. Delete the markers and keep the version you want.
  4. Stage and commit:
git add <filename>
git commit -m "fix: resolve merge conflict"
git push origin main

docs.title: Clean up
docs.text:
Once a branch is merged, you can safely delete it:

git branch -d blog-update

docs.title: Staging and unstaging a File
docs.text:
Staging a specific file 

git add <filename>

Staging everything under git control: 

git add .

Unstaging a file: 

git restore --stage <filename>

docs.title: Committing changes and undoing commits
docs.text:
Commit everything that has been staged 

git commit -m "Commit Message"

Committing a specific file (does not have to be staged yet): 

git commit <filename> -m "Commit Message"

Undoing a commit and unstaging the files last committed. 

git reset HEAD~1

Undoing a commit but leaving the files staged (e.g. to add another file to the same commit). 

git reset --soft HEAD~1

Resetting the directory to the state of the last of the previous commits. This resets the HEAD, unstages any staged files, removes any uncommited changes. It will delete files that were added after the lst previous commit. 

git reset --hard HEAD~1
